sourceMovie.title: American Psycho
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: American Psycho works as a thriller movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The thriller and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Silence of the Lambs
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Silence of the Lambs (1991) belongs to the same conversation as American Psycho — both anchor their thriller in drama emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Jackie Brown
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If American Psycho's thriller stayed with you, Jackie Brown (1997) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The drama through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: The Bone Collector
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where American Psycho excels in thriller, The Bone Collector (1999) doubles down on drama to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: A Walk Among the Tombstones
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: A Walk Among the Tombstones (2014) is the natural next step after American Psycho: same thriller DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and drama elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of American Psycho who respond to its thriller will find The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari (1920) immediately familiar. Both films share a drama sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: A Most Violent Year
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason American Psycho fans keep landing on A Most Violent Year (2014) — the thriller is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Marathon Man
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Marathon Man draws from the same thriller well as American Psycho, channeling drama in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1976 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: The Iceman
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Iceman (2012) captures the thriller appeal of American Psycho without simply imitating it. The drama streak running through both films is what puts The Iceman at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: The Informer
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The thriller appeal of American Psycho — that dark edge, the drama — translates cleanly into The Informer (2019). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Wolf of Wall Street
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Wolf of Wall Street (2013) clearly understood what makes drama work — the same quality that keeps American Psycho rewatchable. Expect the same funny rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

faq.question: What should I watch after American Psycho?
faq.answer: Try The Silence of the Lambs, Jackie Brown, The Bone Collector—each shares DNA with American Psycho while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is American Psycho?
faq.answer: American Psycho (2000) is best described as Thriller, Drama, Crime. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is American Psycho part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "American Psycho Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
